_doc: Exported from Supabase. Do not edit — run npm run macro:export to regenerate.

fashions.realWorldFashionInspiration: Faroese / Icelandic — knitted wool layers, oilskin slickers, practical wind-resistant silhouettes shaped by North Atlantic weather. Blended with fantasy coastal village tropes.

variants.footwear: wrapped hide boots with wool lining
variants.notes: Thick knitted sweater under an oilskin slicker — Faroese layering logic. Trousers tucked into boots. Layers shed easily for work on boats. Sweater patterns are family-specific, like Icelandic lopapeysa.

Same layered silhouette but finer wool with more intricate knit patterns. Wave-pattern embroidery on collar and cuffs marks seniority. The storm hat is earned — only those who've weathered the worst wear one.
Shorter oilskin coat for mobility. Reinforced shoulders with layered hide. Muted colours — no embroidery, nothing to catch on rigging or give you away in fog.
Blue-dyed linen reserved for tide ceremonies. Shell ornaments and kelp garlands. Always barefoot to touch the shore. The blue dye is woad-based — the dyeing itself is part of the rite.

fashions.realWorldFashionInspiration: Georgian / Caucasian highland — chokha coats, fitted waist with cartridge pockets, sheepskin cloaks (nabadi). Mixed with classic fantasy ranger and highland crofter aesthetics.

accessories: broad leather belt with cartridge-style pouches
variants.notes: Fitted coat cinched at the waist with a broad belt — chokha silhouette adapted to fantasy. Cartridge-pocket rows on the chest hold herb vials or fire-strikers. Sheepskin cap stays warm in ridgeline winds.
Darker-dyed coat with a sheepskin cloak (nabadi-style) over the shoulders. Silver throat brooch indicates community standing. The cloak doubles as a sleeping roll on the trail.
Felted gambeson under a leather jerkin — the fitted coat stripped to its martial core. Minimal decoration, terrain-matched colours. Built to fight on slopes and scramble over ridges.

A wide embroidered shoulder drape in bright cream with mountain motifs — worn over the fitted coat. Reserved for solstice and ridge-moot gatherings. The antler circlet is carved from shed antlers, never hunted.

fashions.realWorldFashionInspiration: Korean hanbok-adjacent — clean wrapped lines, high waist ties, seasonal colour coding. Blended with classic fantasy peasant and harvest-village tropes.

variants.notes: Clean-lined wrapped tunic tied at the high waist with a broad sash — hanbok-influenced silhouette in homespun linen. Straw hat is near-universal. Clothes are sun-bleached and patched freely — mending is no shame. Colour of the sash shifts by season.

Same wrapped silhouette but in finer ramie cloth, unpatched. A coloured ribbon on the hat and grain-sheaf embroidery on the apron signal respect. Elders wear muted earth tones — young fieldborn wear brighter seasonal colours.
Fieldborn militia, not professional soldiers. Hardened leather vest over work clothes, sash tucked tight. Still look like farmers at heart — because they are.
Harvest festival dress — flowing white linen gown with golden high-waist sash. Wheat wreaths and wildflower garlands. Barefoot on the earth. The most colour any fieldborn wears all year.

fashions.realWorldFashionInspiration: Byzantine civic dress — layered tunics, brocade, gold-thread embroidery, rigid hierarchy expressed through clothing. Mixed with classic fantasy walled-city and heraldic tropes.

variants.notes: Neat, structured tunic with clean lines — Byzantine-influenced layering without the excess. Everyone looks presentable. Civic pride runs deep — sloppy dress is a social offence inside the walls.
Rich brocade surcoat over fine linen — gold-thread embroidery at collar and cuffs, Byzantine style. Heraldic device on the chest. Rank is readable at a glance; that's the point.
City guard and garrison. Red-and-grey tabard over chain mail. The most uniformed look of any culture — matching is the point. Byzantine tagma discipline meets fantasy city watch.

Long formal robes with gold geometric borders — deliberately archaic, echoing an older imperial style. Worn at council sessions and city holidays. The more old-fashioned it looks, the more authority it carries.

fashions.realWorldFashionInspiration: Tibetan / Bhutanese — heavy wrapped chuba robes, yak-wool layers, felt boots, bold accent colours against earth tones. Blended with classic fantasy dwarf aesthetics — braided beards, rune-work, forge aprons.

variants.footwear: felted wool boots with iron soles
variants.notes: Heavy wrapped coat over wool shirt — chuba-like silhouette cinched with a wide belt that doubles as tool storage. One shoulder often slipped free for forge work. Hair braided tight, beards clasped with stone rings. The wool is dense enough to stop sparks.

variants.footwear: felted wool boots with iron soles
variants.notes: The leather apron worn over the wrapped coat is the mark of craft rank — plain for apprentice, rune-stamped for master. Both shoulders slipped free at the forge; the coat bunches at the waist like a Tibetan work chuba.
Full chainmail over padded coat. Rune-etched helm unique to each clan. Heavy, immovable silhouette — Bergfolk fight like walls. The layering logic is the same as everyday dress, just armoured.

Formal wrapped coat in deep amber and bronze, both shoulders covered. A dark cloth drape over one shoulder pinned with the clan chain. Reserved for oath-taking and ancestor rites in the deep halls. Closest the Bergfolk come to colour — amber against iron-grey.

fashions.realWorldFashionInspiration: Ainu (Hokkaido) — attush bark-cloth robes with bold geometric embroidery, nature-integrated garments, reverence for natural materials. Blended with classic fantasy high-elf aesthetics — flowing, ethereal, organic.

variants.notes: Long bark-cloth robe with bold geometric embroidery at hem and cuffs — Ainu attush influence. The fabric has an organic drape, nothing stiff. Colours shift subtly with the seasons as living-leaf dye fades. The embroidery patterns are believed to ward off corruption.

Elders dress simpler, not grander. Unadorned fabric but finer weave. The circlet is the only marker of age. A single embroidered prayer band on the wrist — the patterns are unique to each elder, never repeated.

Tight-fitting silhouette for canopy movement. Bark armour grown to fit the wearer's body. Camouflage cloak makes them near-invisible in forest. Geometric ward-embroidery concentrated around the heart.

White bark-cloth gowns with the most elaborate embroidery — every edge bordered. Glow faintly at dusk. Worn for equinox gatherings. The flowers mark the specific rite — spring blossoms, autumn berries.

fashions.realWorldFashionInspiration: Romani + punk DIY — layered scavenged fabrics, coin jewelry, bright personal expression from found materials, visible repairs as identity. Mixed with fantasy goblin tinker tropes.

variants.notes: No two goblins dress alike. Clothes are a collage of found materials, traded scraps, and personal trophies. The messier, the more resourceful you look.
Elders pile on more layers and trophies. The tall hat is traditional — taller means you survived longer. Keys symbolise warrens they've overseen.
Strip down for speed. One spiked shoulder pad, war-paint in clan colours. Light and fast — goblin warriors don't stand and fight, they ambush and scatter.

accessories: leather apron covered in burn marks
variants.notes: Goblin tinkers wear their workshop. Apron pockets bulge with gears and screws. Goggles are both tool and status — finer lenses mean better craft.

fashions.realWorldFashionInspiration: Bengali / Filipino — light cotton wraps, woven water-resistant textiles, bare feet or wooden clogs for wading, bright accents against neutral base. Mixed with fantasy scholar-monk aesthetics.

variants.notes: Light cotton wrap tied with a broad sash — Bengali dhoti-influenced draping for ease in humid wetlands. Waterproofed at the hem for wading. Practical but always tidy — Pandor take pride in neatness even knee-deep in the marsh.
Finer silk, darker colours. The jade seal pendant marks a keeper of records. Elders move slowly and dress deliberately — rushing is undignified.
Bamboo-lamellar armour — light and water-resistant. Designed to fight in and around water. Warriors carry nets as readily as blades.
Cloud-white silk robes with hand-painted calligraphy along the hems. Each robe is unique — the text is a personal meditation. Worn for archive openings and water blessings.

fashions.realWorldFashionInspiration: Maasai — ochre body paint, draped shuka cloth, beaded ornament, minimal garments where the body itself is the canvas. Mixed with fantasy giant/titan archetypes.

variants.notes: Giants wear little — a draped hide wrap like a shuka, a shoulder drape in cold weather. Clothing is secondary to ochre body paint and scarification for identity. Beaded stone necklaces mark lineage, Maasai-style.
A great drape of layered hides over both shoulders. The antler crown is ancient — passed down, not made new. Elders move like the landscape.
Warriors strip down and paint up. Each pattern tells a battle. No armour — giant hide is armour enough. Bone bands on the upper arms mark kills.

accessories: full body paint in white clay
variants.notes: Stone masks carved to resemble ancestor faces. Full white clay body paint. Stone chimes at the waist ring with each step. Reserved for the Seven's gathering rites.

fashions.realWorldFashionInspiration: Persian Safavid / Byzantine court — layered silk brocade, rigid dress hierarchy, gold thread, ceremonial excess. Mixed with fantasy dragon-cult and dark imperial aesthetics.

variants.notes: Fitted garments that echo draconic proportions — high Safavid-style collar, tapered sleeves, sharp silhouette. Scale patterns are woven into the brocade, Persian-style. Even common dress carries an imperial weight.

The cape is everything — it mimics folded wings. Crimson and gold brocade, floor-length, lined with black — Safavid court excess filtered through draconic grandeur. The horned diadem recalls ancient dragon crowns.
Armour designed to evoke the dragon form — ridged pauldrons, a back banner that suggests a tail. Intimidation is half the battle.
Full dragon mask of lacquered obsidian. Smoke-purple robes with flame-tongue hems. Ceremonies take place on heated stone floors — barefoot to prove endurance.

fashions.realWorldFashionInspiration: Bedouin / Saharan Tuareg — wrapped head coverings, layered cotton for sun protection, indigo dye, coin and silver ornament, designed for life on the move. Standard fantasy caravan/merchant tropes.

variants.notes: Practical layers that protect from sun and sand. Headscarf doubles as dust mask. Bright colours — caravans want to be seen on the road, not hidden.
The turban's height and embroidery tell how many routes you've mastered. Story-beads — each bead is a journey completed. Elders sit and trade in beads.
Outriders and caravan guards. Fitted for the saddle, loose enough to swing. Chainmail hidden under the headscarf — don't advertise your readiness.
Market festival dress — every coin you've earned displayed as fringe. Saffron and indigo silk. Ceremonies happen on spread rugs — the caravan carries its own sacred ground.

fashions.realWorldFashionInspiration: Polynesian / Samoan — tapa cloth, tattoo-as-dress, shell and bone ornament, minimal garments adapted for water. Mixed with fantasy aquatic/merfolk tropes — bioluminescence, coral-craft.

variants.notes: Merfolk dress for water — minimal coverage, nothing that drags. Kelp wraps at the hips like a Samoan lavalava, coral beads for identity. Tattoo patterns (pe'a-influenced) are as important as clothing — iridescent ink marks lineage and rank.
Elders wear more — a kelp-and-shell drape over the shoulders. Pearl strands mark age. Bioluminescent tattoos glow in deep water for recognition.
Shell-plate armour strapped tight — can't snag on coral. Spine tips on gauntlets double as weapons. Streamlined for combat in currents.
Sea-silk robes that billow like jellyfish in the current. Living coral crown cultivated over years. Worn for reef-song rituals in the deep trenches.

fashions.realWorldFashionInspiration: Turkmen / Kyrgyz — chapan coats, telpek hats, bright embroidered sashes, horse-culture gear. Mixed with fantasy steppe-rider and nomad-warrior tropes.

variants.notes: Wrapped chapan-style coat tied with a broad embroidered sash. Everything designed for the saddle. Telpek-style felt cap stays on in wind. Colours mark family group — everyone in camp can tell kin at a distance.

variants.headwear: tall ceremonial hat with eagle feather
variants.notes: Richly embroidered deel in deeper colours. The eagle feather on the hat is earned, not decorative — one feather per route mastered.
Horsehide lamellar over padded deel. Quiver always on the back. Light enough to ride, tough enough to take an arrow. Wind-spiral painted on the helm.
White and sky-blue robes for sky-song ceremonies. The story-belt has a panel for each generation. Eagle bone whistle calls the wind for blessing.

fashions.realWorldFashionInspiration: Andean / Quechua — layered ponchos, chullo ear-flap caps, altitude-adapted wool layers, bold geometric woven textiles. Mixed with fantasy cliff-dweller and raptor-rider tropes.

variants.notes: Fitted waxed poncho-layer over tight base — Andean altitude logic. Nothing loose to catch the wind at height. Harness is everyday wear, not just for climbing — the eyries demand it. Chullo-style ear-flap caps with feather tokens.
Same tight fit but with fur lining for warmth. Wind-reader chimes at the breast — they read gusts by sound. The feathered cap marks a master of heights.
Glider-cloaks that spread for controlled descents — part clothing, part equipment. Raptor-skull helm for intimidation. Attack from above, always.
The feather cape spread out like wings — each feather is gifted by a different eyrie member. Wind-chime belt sings in the updraft. Ceremonies held at the highest point, open to the sky.

fashions.realWorldFashionInspiration: Amazonian / Yanomami — minimal garments, body paint as primary identity, plant-fibre accessories, deep integration with the forest environment. Mixed with fantasy druid and mycelium-mystic tropes.

variants.notes: Clothes that look half-grown. Moss-cloth tunics that blur the line between garment and undergrowth — Yanomami-level integration with the forest. Body paint in spore-dot patterns is as important as clothing. Always barefoot to feel the forest floor.
The moss crown actually grows on the elder — watered and tended. Mycelium-thread shawl connects them symbolically to the grove network. Spore lantern for underground gatherings.
Thorn-spine armour grown on hardwood frames. Pollen bombs for disorientation. Grove warriors defend by making the forest itself hostile — their gear blends in completely.
Bioluminescent mushroom crowns that glow blue-green in the dark. Ceremonies happen underground at night. Spore-dust body paint makes everyone glow faintly.

fashions.realWorldFashionInspiration: Edo-period Japanese artisan / Ottoman guild — structured work-dress with guild markers, rank expressed through apron and tool quality, clean distinction between master and apprentice. Mixed with fantasy steampunk and artificer tropes.

variants.headwear: brass-rimmed goggles (on forehead or around neck)
variants.notes: Practical workwear that doubles as everyday clothes — no real distinction, like Edo-period artisans who lived in their workshops. Canvas coveralls or fitted jacket and trousers. Always oil-stained. Goggles are identity — guild rank is read from the lens quality.

accessories: master's leather apron with brass guild badge
variants.notes: The guild badge on the apron tells everything — craft, rank, speciality. Multi-lens goggles for detail work. Master artisans are the aristocracy here.
Construct-inspired armour — brass plating over canvas padding. Mechanical gauntlets with enhanced grip. Workshop warriors look like walking machines.

variants.headwear: polished brass crown with clock face
variants.notes: The only time workshop folk wear clean clothes. White canvas coat, polished brass — the clock-face crown marks the turning of a new project cycle. The master-key opens the Great Workshop.

fashions.realWorldFashionInspiration: Tuareg / Berber — indigo-dyed wraps, geometric silver jewelry, veiled faces, desert elegance in alien materials. Mixed with fantasy fey-court and Art Nouveau aesthetics.

variants.notes: Gossamer wraps draped Tuareg-style — the crystal thread refracts light like indigo dye shifts in sun. Geometric silver crystal-clasps at shoulder and hip. No two garments look the same in different light. Unsettling to outsiders, like Tuareg blue-stained skin but with prismatic light.
The tiara resonates with the elder's voice — it hums when they speak truth. Layered crystal shards click and chime with movement. Fae elders sound like wind chimes.
Crystal shards embedded in armour — they ring on impact, disorienting opponents with harmonic feedback. The sound of a Fae warrior approaching is the warning itself.
Full prismatic regalia — the crown splits light into rainbows. Light-woven cape actually emits soft light. Ceremonies are visual symphonies, impossible to describe to non-Fae.

fashions.realWorldFashionInspiration: Ethiopian ecclesiastical / Coptic — wrapped white shamma cloth, hidden symbolism, archival and monastic dress traditions. Mixed with fantasy post-apocalyptic and ancient-guardian tropes.

variants.notes: Layers of salvaged fabric wrapped shamma-style, patched but never discarded. Hoods always available — Remnants prefer shadow, like Coptic monks withdrawing from the world. Breathing masks for venturing into unstable ruins. The visible repairs are intentional — each patch is a small history, a record kept in cloth.
The metal half-mask is inscribed with archive glyphs — it's both identity and rank. Memory shard chain holds fragments of pre-collapse knowledge. Elders are living libraries.
Heavy salvaged armour — mismatched plates bolted together. Function over aesthetics. Refuge warriors guard the perimeter against corruption and scavengers.

accessories: memory shard held in both hands
variants.notes: Full featureless white mask and shroud — Ethiopian timkat-procession stillness. Identity dissolved into the collective memory. Memory shards held aloft during remembrance rites. Silence is the ceremony.

fashions.realWorldFashionInspiration: Mediterranean maritime — Greek island / Dalmatian coast — sun-bleached linen, rope belts, open collars, relaxed practical layers for warm coastal life. Mixed with fantasy port-town and sailor tropes.

variants.notes: Loose linen shirt with open collar, vest with shell buttons, rolled trousers — Greek island fisherman meets fantasy dockworker. Everything salt-faded and soft from washing. Bandana keeps hair out of rigging. Relaxed and sun-worn — harbor folk don't dress to impress.
The captain's hat doesn't mean ship captain — it's a harbor elder's mark. Tide-chart scarf printed with local currents. Spyglass is ceremonial now but always carried.
Harbor guard and anti-piracy patrol. Fitted for climbing and boarding. Cutlass and hook — weapons that work in tight ship corridors. No armour — it drowns you.
Tide festival dress — white flowing robes, barefoot on the docks. Net-weave shawl symbolises the catch. Lanterns lit at dusk and floated out to sea for the lost.

fashions.realWorldFashionInspiration: Edo-period Japanese foundry workers / Ottoman guild metalworkers — structured workwear with branded guild marks, rank through tool quality and apron insignia. Mixed with fantasy living-construct and iron-golem aesthetics.

variants.headwear: none or cloth wrap under work helm
variants.notes: Steinfolk dress like they never left the forge. Oil-treated canvas over thick undergarments. Iron plating on boots and shoulders is everyday wear — the weight is nothing to stone-folk.
The crucible brand is earned — literally branded when you pour your first independent melt. Forge visor never comes off during work. The apron has survived more heat than most buildings.

variants.headwear: full iron helm with chain drape
variants.notes: Walking fortresses. Full iron — helm, chain, plate, greaves. Steinfolk warriors don't dodge; they absorb. The chain drape from the helm protects the neck and looks like a metal beard.

variants.headwear: polished iron crown with forge-light inset
variants.notes: Same silhouette as everyday — Steinfolk don't do flowing robes. Instead, everything is polished mirror-bright. The forge-light inset in the crown glows orange. The ceremonial hammer has never struck metal.

fashions.realWorldFashionInspiration: Dayak (Borneo) — woven rattan, intricate beadwork, hornbill feather motifs, jungle-fitted garments that integrate with dense vegetation. Mixed with fantasy wild-elf and thorn-fey tropes.

variants.headwear: none — hair woven with thorns and leaves
variants.notes: Tight-fitting clothes for moving through dense undergrowth — Dayak jungle logic. Thorn-woven fibre is naturally barbed on the outside, like rattan armour that discourages grabbing. Intricate beadwork at wrists and throat. Hair is part of the outfit — woven with the thicket itself.
Elders slow down and dress in layers — they sit in the thicket's heart and the thicket grows around them. The thorn-and-web veil is half-natural, half-made. Web face paint marks the weaver-speakers.
Almost invisible in the thicket. Thorn spikes grown directly into braided hair. Thorn-gauntlets for close combat. The insect-swarm gourd releases stinging clouds — thicket warfare is nasty.

variants.headwear: living flower crown with bioluminescent moths
variants.notes: Spider-silk robes shimmer in moonlight like dew-covered webs. Living moths perch on the flower crown, wings glowing. Ceremonies happen at midnight in the deepest thicket — outsiders are never invited.
